items.passage: Lithium-ion batteries power most modern phones, laptops, and electric vehicles around the world. During charging, lithium ions move through a liquid and settle inside the negative electrode for storage. When the device runs, those ions flow back toward the positive side and release useful electrical energy. Engineers value these batteries because they hold a large charge while staying relatively light and compact. However, repeated cycles slowly damage the internal materials, which gradually reduces how much energy the cell can store.

Kinship systems organize how people in a society trace family ties and assign social duties. In many cultures, descent is followed mainly through the father, while others emphasize the mother instead. These rules shape who may marry whom and how property passes between successive generations. Anthropologists argue that kinship once provided early communities with reliable support during periods of hardship. Although modern states now handle many of these roles, kinship still strongly influences daily life across the globe.

The periodic table arranges every known chemical element according to its atomic number and properties. Elements in the same vertical column tend to behave similarly because they share comparable outer electron arrangements. This pattern allowed early chemists to predict the existence of elements that had not yet been discovered. Metals occupy most of the table, while nonmetals cluster toward the upper right region of the chart. Understanding these relationships helps scientists explain why certain substances react readily and others remain stable.

The Doppler effect describes how the observed frequency of a wave changes when its source moves. When a speeding ambulance nears a listener, its siren sounds higher because the sound waves bunch together. After the vehicle passes, the waves stretch apart and the perceived pitch suddenly drops lower. Astronomers apply the identical principle to light, measuring whether distant galaxies travel toward or away from Earth. This characteristic shift in starlight became one of the strongest clues that the universe is steadily expanding.

Comparative advantage explains why nations gain by trading even when one produces everything more efficiently. A country should concentrate on goods that it makes at the lowest relative cost compared with others. By specializing this way, it frees scarce labor and resources for the activities where it performs best. Trading partners then exchange surplus output, allowing both sides to consume more than they could alone. Economists regard this insight as one of the central arguments in favor of open international markets.

Linear perspective gave Renaissance painters a method for showing depth on a flat surface convincingly. Artists drew lines that appear to converge toward a single distant point on the horizon. Objects placed closer to that vanishing point were painted smaller, which imitated how human vision works. The architect Brunelleschi demonstrated the technique in Florence, and other painters quickly adopted his careful rules. As a result, pictures gained a striking realism that earlier flat medieval images had clearly lacked.
